questions.q: What is the difference between an adjacency list and an adjacency matrix?
questions.a: An adjacency list stores neighbors per node; a matrix stores edge presence in a 2D grid.

questions.q: Why is an adjacency list preferred for sparse graphs?
questions.a: It uses less memory and iterates only over real edges.

questions.q: When would an adjacency matrix be the better choice?
questions.a: When the graph is dense or you need constant-time edge existence checks.

questions.q: What does BFS guarantee on an unweighted graph?
questions.a: The shortest path in number of edges.

questions.q: Why does BFS use a queue?
questions.a: It explores nodes in layers, so the first time you reach a node is via the fewest edges.

questions.q: When should BFS be your first choice?
questions.a: When all edges have equal weight and you need minimum-hop reachability or levels.

questions.q: What is DFS especially good for?
questions.a: Exploring depth, backtracking, and component structure.

questions.q: Why is DFS often implemented with recursion or a stack?
questions.a: The call stack naturally tracks the path you are exploring.

questions.q: When would DFS be better than BFS?
questions.a: When you need exhaustive path exploration, cycle detection, or topological reasoning.

questions.q: What key constraint does Dijkstra require?
questions.a: All edge weights must be nonnegative.

questions.q: Why does Dijkstra fail with negative edges?
questions.a: Its greedy choice can be invalidated after a later negative-weight relaxation.

questions.q: When should you use Dijkstra instead of Bellman-Ford?
questions.a: When the graph has nonnegative weights and you want faster shortest-path computation.

questions.q: What can Bellman-Ford handle that Dijkstra cannot?
questions.a: Negative edge weights and negative cycle detection.

questions.q: Why is Bellman-Ford slower than Dijkstra?
questions.a: It relaxes all edges repeatedly, typically up to V - 1 passes.

questions.q: When is Bellman-Ford the right tool?
questions.a: When negative weights exist or you need to detect whether a negative cycle is reachable.
An ordering of nodes in a DAG where every edge points forward.
It gives a valid dependency order for builds, scheduling, and compilation steps.
When the graph has a cycle, because no linear order can satisfy all dependencies.
